key_insight: A stepped, semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC interface with nanoscale Ni enrichment enables stable plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine eutectic by promoting dislocation activity, twinning, and interfacial shear instead of brittle cracking.
engineering_relevance: Designing eutectic or heterostructured alloys with controlled interface spacing and chemistry can raise strength while preserving ductility, which is valuable for additively manufactured structural materials.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion to create a lamellar Cr-Ni eutectic microstructure
Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM to resolve interface structure, orientation relationship, and local chemistry
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension, paired with TEM, to track deformation mechanisms
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an average interlamellar spacing of about 450 nm and in situ tests showing high flow stress with large plastic strain without cracking, supported by microscopy-based mechanism mapping.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated in a specific additively manufactured Cr-Ni eutectic, so transferability to other compositions, interface spacings, or loading conditions should be validated before generalizing.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
